questions.specification:
**Source 1:**
Researchers surveyed 800 university students (200 from each of four cultural groups: Sweden, Japan, Brazil, and Kenya). Students rated the question “How much do you trust a close friend?” on a 10-point scale. Responses of 8–10 were categorized as indicating “high trust.”
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/90f52291-8c51-47fc-a7a9-66771cb3ae21)

**Source 2:**
Researchers were interested in investigating how adults from different cultural backgrounds experience and describe interpersonal trust. They conducted semi-structured interviews with 15 participants (8 from collectivist backgrounds and 7 from individualist backgrounds). The interviews were recorded and transcribed, and the transcripts were subjected to thematic analysis by two independent researchers. The researchers first identified categories (themes) separately, then compared their coding, and finally re-coded the transcripts together.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a9d0f7c3-ac26-40ba-862f-b3a5939027b7)

**Source 3:**
An undergraduate student was interested in exploring differences in reported trust levels between students from individualist and collectivist cultural backgrounds. They recruited 50 participants from their own university (aged 18–22). Participants were asked two questions:
“Rate your general trust in others on a scale from 1 to 10.”
“State your cultural background.”
Based on self-reported backgrounds, 28 participants were categorized as individualist and 22 as collectivist. The researcher summarized the data as follows:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4dda3468-babb-4d96-a449-556c10168c27)

**Source 4:**
In a survey of 120 adult participants, aged between 30 and 50, (40 each from the USA, Japan, and Mexico), researchers administered two standardized scales: Collectivism Scale (1–7), wherein higher scores indicate stronger collectivist orientation, and the General Trust Scale (1–10) where higher scores indicate greater general trust in others. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0d072ec5-bab6-4a82-8e31-f0106aa544be)
*r(118) = 0.47, p < 0.01*

**Source 5:**
Researchers tested 120 participants (60 collectivist background, 60 individualist background) in a trust game with real money. Each participant received £10 and could choose how much to share with a partner. 

Condition 1: Partner described as an “in-group” member. 

Condition 2: Partner described as randomly assigned.

The researchers found that collectivist participants shared significantly more with in-group members (mean = £4.80). On the other hand, individualist participants shared more with randomly assigned partners (mean = £5.10). Overall sharing did not differ significantly across groups, but it is suggested that cultural framing influenced the trigger of trust.

parts.content: Explain one issue that limits the interpretation of the data in **Source 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a limitation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why it limits interpretation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicitly linking to claim.

Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Cultural reporting bias – Japanese participants may underreport high trust due to cultural modesty norms → numbers may not reflect actual trust levels.
Sampling limitation – Only university students; trust patterns may differ in older or less-educated populations.
Cross-sectional design – Snapshot only, cannot show how culture shapes trust over time.
Correlation not causation – Can’t conclude that culture causes differences in trust.

parts.content: Analyse the findings from **Source 2** and state a conclusion linked to the claim that cultural values influence levels of trust in interpersonal relationships.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for correct analysis, clear conclusion and explicit links to claim.

1–2 marks: Descriptive summary of one or two themes without analysis.

3–4 marks: Identification and explanation of how the themes link to claim.

5–6 marks: Well-developed analysis linking multiple themes to claim. Analysis considers multiple perspectives; is well-balanced. 

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Reciprocity theme suggests collectivist trust relies on obligations being met.
Transparency highlights individualist value of open self-disclosure.
In-group preference supports SIT (social identity theory) → trust stronger within group boundaries.
Ambiguity tolerance differences show culture shapes thresholds for trust.
Conclusion: Cultural values influence how trust is expressed and developed, not necessarily overall levels.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could avoid bias in the study described in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for identification of bias, explanation of effects, and valid strategies. 

1–2 marks: Simple identification of bias.

3–4 marks: Some explanation of how bias affects results.

5–6 marks: Clear, well-developed discussion of strategies to minimise bias.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Researchers personally knew participants → social desirability bias.
All from one university → sampling bias.
Self-reported trust → measurement bias.
Strategies: anonymous surveys, random sampling across institutions, validated scales, third-party data collection.

parts.content: To what extent are the findings of **Source 3** transferable to other populations or contexts?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of transferability and acknowledgement of both values and limitations. 

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Limited: sample only 18–22, one university, Western academic context → cannot generalise.
Cultural diversity in sample may help transferability within similar universities.
Not transferable to non-student or rural populations.
Contextual limits: social media/academic pressures may affect trust differently than in older populations.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could improve the credibility of the findings in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of credibility and suggestions for improvement.

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Improve with triangulation (surveys + interviews).
Reflexivity: researcher acknowledging their cultural bias.
Peer debriefing → reduces subjective interpretation.
Using established trust scales → improves measurement validity.

parts.content:
Using at least three of the sources above (sources 1-5), and your own knowledge, answer the following question:
To what extent can we conclude that cultural values play a role in shaping interpersonal trust?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for a well-developed response with critical analysis, clear links between multiple sources and the claim, and a consistent conclusion.

1–3 marks: Very limited knowledge and understanding of the sources and/or the claim. Minimal or inaccurate reference to sources. Largely descriptive, with little or no analysis. Psychological terminology absent or misused.

4–6 marks: Some knowledge and understanding of the claim. Reference to at least one source, but not always accurate or integrated. Limited analysis, with few links between sources and the claim. Some appropriate psychological terminology, but often vague.

7–9 marks: Reasonable knowledge and understanding of the claim, with reference to at least two sources. Some analysis and evaluation of research, though uneven or lacking development. Links between sources are attempted but may be simplistic. Psychological terminology is mostly accurate.

10–12 marks: Clear knowledge and understanding of the claim. Effective use of at least three sources, accurately applied to the question. Analysis and evaluation are present, with some critical thinking. Sources are compared or synthesised in addressing the claim. Psychological terminology is used appropriately.

13–15 marks: Comprehensive knowledge and understanding of the claim. Well-developed, balanced evaluation of the claim using at least three sources. Clear integration of sources with additional relevant knowledge. Critical analysis consistently developed (e.g., weighing strengths/limitations, contrasting findings, discussing generalisability). A well-reasoned and consistent conclusion. Precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Support for claim: **Source 2** (interviews: culture affects basis of trust), **Source 4** (correlation collectivism–trust), **Source 5** (different triggers in trust game).
Challenges to claim: **Source 1** (differences could be reporting norms, not real trust); **Source 3** (differences small and student-led).
Own knowledge: Hofstede’s cultural dimensions (individualism vs collectivism), SIT, cultural psychology findings on in-group/out-group trust.
Concepts: Bias (social desirability in self-reports), Causality (correlation not causation), Perspective (emic vs etic approaches).
Conclusion: Evidence suggests cultural values shape the expression and triggers of trust, but caution: not universal, findings limited to specific groups.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of measurement applies to research on hormones in the context of human relationships.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of measurement or hormones.
Mentions hormones vaguely (e.g., testosterone, oxytocin).
Weak links to social behaviour.
Minimal or no evaluation of measurement methods.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of measurement in hormone research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Baumgartner et al. on oxytocin, Dabbs on testosterone and aggression).
Explains how hormone levels are measured (blood/saliva tests, intranasal administration).
Some evaluation of reliability and ecological validity.
Partial conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of measurement in hormone research.
Multiple studies explained (Baumgartner, Kosfeld, Dabbs).
Well-developed evaluation:
Strengths (controlled administration, objective assays).
Limitations (individual differences, ecological validity, ethics).
Clear analysis of implications for understanding social behaviour.
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

questions.specification:
Okafor and Stein (2012) conducted an experimental study on whether oxytocin influences cooperation in social interactions. The aim was to test if oxytocin administration increases trust and cooperative behaviour.

Eighty participants were randomly assigned to inhale either oxytocin or a placebo before engaging in a series of trust games. In these games, Player 1 was given a sum of money and could choose how much to send to Player 2, knowing that the amount sent would be tripled. Player 2 then decided how much to return to Player 1. Cooperation was measured by how much participants sent and returned.

Results showed that participants who received oxytocin sent significantly more money and returned larger amounts, suggesting enhanced cooperation. However, cultural differences emerged: the effect was stronger in participants from collectivist backgrounds than in individualist ones. 

[Note: Fictionalised but draws on Kosfeld et al. (2005) and later cultural studies.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: causality, measurement, perspective, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Causality: Experimental design supports hormone → behaviour link, but effects vary.
Measurement: Trust game artificial—ecological validity issues.
Perspective: Stronger effect in collectivist cultures highlights cultural framing.
Responsibility: Administering hormones to healthy participants raises ethical concerns.

questions.specification: A couples therapist asks clients to describe how they form first impressions of potential partners. Many report relying on quick judgments rather than carefully evaluating all the available information.

parts.content: Explain **one** cognitive bias relevant to decision-making.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a cognitive bias and application to the scenario.

1–2 marks: Limited description of a cognitive bias (e.g., vague mention of heuristics). Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description (e.g., confirmation bias, availability heuristic, anchoring). Partial application to first impressions.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed description of a cognitive bias with well-developed application to how clients make rapid judgments about partners. Terminology used correctly.

**Acceptable responses:**
Confirmation bias → people notice traits that support first impressions.
Availability heuristic → judgments based on salient information.
Halo effect → **one** positive trait influences the whole impression.

questions.specification:
**Source 1:**
A team of researchers who were interested in prosocial behaviour wanted to investigate whether intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation influenced helping behaviour. They recruited 180 participants from an ad in the newspaper, the adults were randomly assigned to three conditions (N = 60 per group):

Intrinsic prime: Participants wrote about values that made them feel connected to others.

Extrinsic prime: Participants wrote about rewards they hoped to earn in the future.

Control: Participants began the experiment with an unrelated writing task. 

The participants were not informed about the actual aim of the study, they believed the writing task was the extent of the experiment. As the participants left the room, they observed a staged event in which a confederate “accidentally” dropped a stack of books. The key measure was whether participants helped pick them up.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1be9877a-94b5-4e6f-8f72-d2aa64786045)

**Source 2:**
Psychologists at a certain research institute were interested in exploring what motivates long-term caregiving in familial relationships. The researching team reached out to a local medical center to create a sample of adults who were either: a) parents of disabled children, or b) adult children caring for elderly children. The sample was comprised of 20 participants. Researchers carried out semi-structured interviews with each participant, recording and transcribing the interactions. The transcripts underwent thematic analysis.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8235d332-e64f-4d52-93f5-2ac5c11ee622)

**Source 3:**
An undergraduate student studying psychology conducted a survey as part of an assignment. They wanted to examine why students help their fellow peers. The researcher chose to survey students outside their own psychology class. The final sample included 60 classmates, aged between 18-20. The student researcher administered a survey asking students the open-ended question: “Why do you help your friends?” The researcher then coded the responses into three categories of motivation:

Intrinsic motivation: Responses that suggest individuals help because *they want to*.

Reciprocity: Responses that suggest individuals help *expecting help in return.*

Emotional reward: Responses that suggest individuals help because *it feels good*.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/54ac9c13-fede-45b6-80d6-57d380b4c078)

**Source 4:** Researchers in a university psychology department were in the process of putting together a book about altruism and prosocial behaviour. As part of their publication, they included a study wherein they tested whether intrinsic motivation predicted real-world social behaviour. The sample of the study included 250 university students, spread across years and ranging from 17 to 23 years old. The researchers determined the intrinsic motivation of individuals by administering a standardised test that quantified the level of intrinsic motivation on a scale (1-7). Researchers also asked individuals to report the average number of hours they spent volunteering per month. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e2de32cc-4eb3-4e8d-bcc8-01b59b035274)
*r(248) = 0.623, p < 0.05*

**Source 5:** 
Researchers wanted to conduct an experiment to test whether extrinsic social recognition influenced charitable giving. Unfortunately, the sample chosen by the researchers consisted of second-year psychology students, as these were the only individuals who had time to participate in the study. The participants were randomly assigned two one of two conditions:

Public condition: Participants told that names of donors displayed on a visible donor list.

Private condition: Participants told that donations were made anonymously.

On tablet screens, participants in each condition were asked if they would donate money to the cause, and how much (scale of £1 to £10)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b8415d33-34f8-4c0d-8184-b69dcf3f7b8d)

parts.content: Explain one issue that limits the interpretation of the data in **Source 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a limitation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why it limits interpretation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicitly linking to claim.

Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Helping task may lack ecological validity — picking up books may not reflect real-life helping.
Short-term priming doesn’t capture long-term motivation.
Cultural bias — sample only one country, helping norms differ elsewhere.
Correlational — doesn’t prove motivation caused helping behaviour.

parts.content: Analyse the findings from **Source 2** and state a conclusion linked to the claim that motivation plays a role in prosocial behaviour within human relationships.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for correct analysis, clear conclusion and explicit links to claim.

1–2 marks: Descriptive summary of one or two themes without analysis.

3–4 marks: Identification and explanation of how the themes link to claim.

5–6 marks: Well-developed analysis linking multiple themes to claim. Analysis considers multiple perspectives; is well-balanced. 

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Themes show intrinsic motivations (love, responsibility) strongly linked with prosocial behaviour.
Extrinsic obligation suggests cultural/social pressure also shapes behaviour.
Emotional reward indicates overlap between intrinsic and extrinsic.
Conclusion: Prosocial behaviour in close relationships is largely intrinsically motivated, but social norms and emotional rewards also play a role.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could avoid bias in the study described in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for identification of bias, explanation of effects, and valid strategies. 

1–2 marks: Simple identification of bias.

3–4 marks: Some explanation of how bias affects results.

5–6 marks: Clear, well-developed discussion of strategies to minimise bias.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Researchers coded responses themselves → confirmation bias.
Participants were classmates → demand/social desirability bias.
Strategies: use independent coders, anonymised surveys, broader sampling, survey with close-ended questions.

parts.content: To what extent are the findings of **Source 3** transferable to other populations or contexts?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of transferability and acknowledgement of both values and limitations. 

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Limited: only university students (young adults, same environment).
May not transfer to older adults, professional caregiving, or cross-cultural contexts.
Some transferability within similar peer-group settings.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could improve the credibility of the findings in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of credibility and suggestions for improvement.

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Use triangulation (surveys + interviews).
Reflexivity: researcher acknowledging their own bias.
Peer review or intercoder agreement for coding categories.

parts.content:
Using at least three of the sources in the resource booklet (sources 1-5), and your own knowledge, answer the following question:
To what extent can we conclude that motivation plays a role in prosocial behaviour within human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for a well-developed response with critical analysis, clear links between multiple sources and the claim, and a consistent conclusion.

1–3 marks: Very limited knowledge and understanding of the sources and/or the claim. Minimal or inaccurate reference to sources. Largely descriptive, with little or no analysis. Psychological terminology absent or misused.

4–6 marks: Some knowledge and understanding of the claim. Reference to at least one source, but not always accurate or integrated. Limited analysis, with few links between sources and the claim. Some appropriate psychological terminology, but often vague.

7–9 marks: Reasonable knowledge and understanding of the claim, with reference to at least two sources. Some analysis and evaluation of research, though uneven or lacking development. Links between sources are attempted but may be simplistic. Psychological terminology is mostly accurate.

10–12 marks: Clear knowledge and understanding of the claim. Effective use of at least three sources, accurately applied to the question. Analysis and evaluation are present, with some critical thinking. Sources are compared or synthesised in addressing the claim. Psychological terminology is used appropriately.

13–15 marks: Comprehensive knowledge and understanding of the claim. Well-developed, balanced evaluation of the claim using at least three sources. Clear integration of sources with additional relevant knowledge. Critical analysis consistently developed (e.g., weighing strengths/limitations, contrasting findings, discussing generalisability). A well-reasoned and consistent conclusion. Precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Support: **Source 1** (intrinsic prime → more helping), **Source 2** (caregivers motivated by love), **Source 4** (intrinsic motivation correlated with volunteering).
Challenges: **Source 5** shows extrinsic/public rewards also drive prosocial behaviour; **Source 3** limited by bias, and so is 5 (sampling bias). Participants may not have donated, in **Source 5**, because they did not identify with the cause.
Own knowledge: Empathy-altruism theory (Batson), self-determination theory, reciprocity norms.
Concepts: Causality (intrinsic vs extrinsic overlap), Perspective (individual vs cultural interpretations of “duty”), Bias (self-report limitations).
Conclusion: Evidence suggests motivation strongly shapes prosocial behaviour, but both intrinsic and extrinsic forms are influential depending on context.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of responsibility apply to research on stereotypes in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of responsibility in research.
Mentions stereotypes vaguely (e.g., “stereotypes are bad”) with no detail.
Little or no relevant research (e.g., Steele & Aronson).
Weak or no link to human relationships (trust, discrimination, prejudice).

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of responsibility in stereotype research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Steele & Aronson on stereotype threat).
Identifies ethical issues (e.g., reinforcing negative stereotypes, participant distress).
Some discussion of how responsibility matters for human relationships (e.g., stereotype threat affects performance and can harm social interactions).
Some evaluation of methods, but not fully developed.
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of responsibility in stereotype research.
Multiple studies integrated (Steele & Aronson, Spencer et al., Croizet & Claire).
Clear analysis of responsibility: researchers must minimise harm, avoid reinforcing harmful stereotypes, and consider real-world impact on relationships (e.g., workplace discrimination, educational inequality).
Well-developed evaluation: strengths (controlled experiments), but limitations (risk of stigmatization, ethical concerns).
Explicit link to human relationships: stereotypes affect trust, belonging, and social outcomes, making researcher responsibility central.
Balanced conclusion linking responsibility to ethical and social implications of stereotype research.

parts.content: Evaluate how the concept of bias affects studies of eyewitness testimony in the context of human relationships.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of bias in eyewitness testimony.
Mentions memory distortion vaguely.
Weak or no reference to studies.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of bias in eyewitness research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Loftus & Palmer car crash, Yuille & Cutshall real eyewitnesses).
Identifies bias sources (leading questions, demand characteristics).
Some evaluation (ecological validity, artificiality).
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of bias in eyewitness research.
Multiple studies (Loftus & Palmer, Yuille & Cutshall, Loftus & Pickrell on false memories).
Well-developed evaluation of biases: experimenter bias, ecological validity, population samples.
Clear analysis of real-world implications for relationships (trust, justice system).
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

questions.specification:
Kumar and Delgado (2019) examined predictors of divorce in a 10-year longitudinal study of married couples. Their aim was to identify whether communication patterns and stress regulation during conflicts predicted long-term relationship outcomes.

The study followed 150 couples from early marriage over a decade. Every two years, couples completed questionnaires about marital satisfaction and stress levels. In addition, they participated in videotaped conflict-resolution tasks where they discussed ongoing disagreements while researchers coded their interactions for hostility, defensiveness, and problem-solving strategies. Salivary cortisol samples were collected to measure physiological stress.

Results indicated that couples who displayed high levels of hostility and poor stress regulation during conflict were significantly more likely to divorce within ten years. By contrast, couples who used positive communication strategies and showed stable cortisol levels were more likely to remain married. Researchers noted some attrition; many couples dropped out.

[Note: Fictionalised but draws on Gottman’s research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Couples who dropped out skew results; survivors may not represent divorcing couples.
Causality: Conflict observed predicts divorce, but other factors (e.g., finances) unmeasured.
Change: Communication patterns evolve—snapshot coding may miss long-term shifts.
Responsibility: Recording intimate conflicts raises privacy issues; stress during tasks may harm couples.

questions.specification:
**Source 1:**
A clinic that specialised in couples therapy enlisted a team of researchers to explore predictors of relationship satisfaction in young adult couples. The researcher team was curious about the connection between the frequency of daily texting and/or instant messaging and relationship satisfaction. The participants included over 400 young adults in long-term romantic relationships, enrolled through an online ad; the age of participants varied between 18 and 30. Individuals filled out a virtual questionnaire that asked them to report the average number of messages exchanged daily with their partner, and relationship satisfaction on a 1–10 scale (scale was on a slider that allowed decimal values).
Based on message frequency, participants were grouped into three categories:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/75cfaec9-da17-43c0-9ce6-a6d130b573fa)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/16895a0b-ca05-4538-a9a6-406548e39767)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/34df249a-8927-47cf-bc54-bff4920dff05)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/221bc7ad-e10f-48b3-bf6a-5c76da48dce8)

**Source 2:** 
The same team of researchers mentioned in **Source 1** also conducted a qualitative study on how couples experience technology in their relationships. The researchers randomly picked 15 couples from online volunteers, ages of the individuals varied from 25 to 40. Each couple participated in semi-structed interviews with a researcher. Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed and analysed between researchers for recurring themes.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3cd922f7-b53e-4b23-83f8-17160fbefb48)

**Source 3** 
An undergraduate student with an interest in technology and the psychology of human relationships wanted to explore whether friendships formed online would be considered as important as offline ones. The sample included 50 university students, all peers or friends, that were aged between 18 and 22. The student researcher sent the participants a short survey that asked: a) “Do you have any “close” online friends?” and b) “Are your online friendships as important as your offline ones?” The open-ended responses were coded by the student researcher into categories like “Yes” and “No”. The researcher discovered the following: 62% of the sample reported having at least one close online friend, and of those, 48% said their online friendships were as important as offline ones.

**Source 4**
A large-scale correlational study was conducted with the intention of examining whether time spent on social media was related to loneliness. The study consisted of 600 individuals, spanning between 16 and 30; the sample consisted of individuals who frequented the campus wherein this study took place. Participants self-reported the daily hours spent on social media, and also completed the UCLA Loneliness Scale (higher scores = greater loneliness, scale of 20-80).
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/65449aeb-e4ea-4f2b-8590-4d2b6ab41925)
*r= 0.598, p < 0.05*

**Source 5**
An experiment investigated how the presence of a mobile phone affected interpersonal conversation. The sample consisted of 120 adults (over the age of 18) who were strangers to each other and paired randomly. Each pair engaged in 10 minutes of conversation before being asked to rate the quality of the conversation on a scale of 1-10. Pairs were randomly assigned to one of two conditions: 

Phone present: Participants were allowed to have phones on the table, and look at them at any time if they wanted to.

No phone: No phones were present.

Researchers found that in the “phone present” condition, the mean rating of conversations was 6.1. In the “no phone” condition, the mean rating was 7.8.

parts.content: Explain one issue that limits the interpretation of the data in **Source 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a limitation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why it limits interpretation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicitly linking to claim.

Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Histogram shows correlations, not causation → satisfaction may affect messaging, not vice versa.
Data self-reported → social desirability bias.
Sample only young adults → not generalisable to older couples.
Frequency doesn’t capture quality of messages.

parts.content: Analyse the findings from **Source 2** and state a conclusion linked to the claim that technology influences relationship quality.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for correct analysis, clear conclusion and explicit links to claim.

1–2 marks: Descriptive summary of one or two themes without analysis.

3–4 marks: Identification and explanation of how the themes link to claim.

5–6 marks: Well-developed analysis linking multiple themes to claim. Analysis considers multiple perspectives; is well-balanced. 

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Technology increases connection when balanced.
Can create conflict when overused or when expectations differ.
Couples negotiate boundaries, suggesting an active role in shaping relationship quality.
Conclusion: Technology influences relationships, but effects depend on how it is managed.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could avoid bias in the study described in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for identification of bias, explanation of effects, and valid strategies. 

1–2 marks: Simple identification of bias.

3–4 marks: Some explanation of how bias affects results.

5–6 marks: Clear, well-developed discussion of strategies to minimise bias.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Researcher surveyed friends → sampling bias.
Self-report → demand characteristics.
Strategy: anonymous surveys, larger sample, independent coder.

parts.content: To what extent are the findings of **Source 3** transferable to other populations or contexts?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of transferability and acknowledgement of both values and limitations. 

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Limited to university peers (same age, similar lifestyle).
May not transfer to older generations or long-distance couples.
Some transferability to similar youth populations.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could improve the credibility of the findings in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of credibility and suggestions for improvement.

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Use triangulation (surveys + interviews + behavioural measures).
Reflexivity: researcher acknowledges their own tech use bias.
Peer review of coding.

parts.content:
Using at least three of the sources in the resource booklet (sources 1-5), and your own knowledge, answer the following question:
To what extent can we conclude that technology influences relationship quality?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for a well-developed response with critical analysis, clear links between multiple sources and the claim, and a consistent conclusion.

1–3 marks: Very limited knowledge and understanding of the sources and/or the claim. Minimal or inaccurate reference to sources. Largely descriptive, with little or no analysis. Psychological terminology absent or misused.

4–6 marks: Some knowledge and understanding of the claim. Reference to at least one source, but not always accurate or integrated. Limited analysis, with few links between sources and the claim. Some appropriate psychological terminology, but often vague.

7–9 marks: Reasonable knowledge and understanding of the claim, with reference to at least two sources. Some analysis and evaluation of research, though uneven or lacking development. Links between sources are attempted but may be simplistic. Psychological terminology is mostly accurate.

10–12 marks: Clear knowledge and understanding of the claim. Effective use of at least three sources, accurately applied to the question. Analysis and evaluation are present, with some critical thinking. Sources are compared or synthesised in addressing the claim. Psychological terminology is used appropriately.

13–15 marks: Comprehensive knowledge and understanding of the claim. Well-developed, balanced evaluation of the claim using at least three sources. Clear integration of sources with additional relevant knowledge. Critical analysis consistently developed (e.g., weighing strengths/limitations, contrasting findings, discussing generalisability). A well-reasoned and consistent conclusion. Precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Support: **Source 1** (messaging frequency linked to satisfaction), **Source 2** (themes of connection/conflict) + researcher triangulation during analysis, **Source 5** (phone presence reduces quality).
Challenges: **Source 3** biased student project and how do we define what a “close” friend is, **Source 4** shows higher social media use linked to more loneliness. **Source 5** - the lower quality may just be because participants are strangers.
Own knowledge: Displacement hypothesis (tech reduces face-to-face interaction), Media multiplexity theory, social compensation vs rich-get-richer hypotheses.
Conclusion: Technology shapes relationship quality, but direction depends on context, type of use, and individual differences.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of measurement applies to social identity theory research in the context of human relationships.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of measurement or SIT.
Mentions in-group/out-group but vaguely, without research detail.
Response descriptive, minimal connection to human relationships.
Little or no evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of SIT and measurement.
Refers to at least one relevant study (e.g., Tajfel’s minimal group experiments, Cialdini et al. on basking in reflected glory).
Explains how group identity and bias are measured (e.g., allocation of points, behavioural indicators).
Some evaluation of validity or ecological issues.
Conclusion present but underdeveloped.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of SIT and measurement.
Accurate use of multiple studies (Tajfel, Cialdini, Abrams et al.).
Clear explanation of methods used to operationalize identity and bias.
Well-developed evaluation (strengths of experimental control, limits of artificiality, cultural variation).
Accurate psychological terminology, reasoned and balanced conclusion.

parts.content: To what extent does change explain cultural influences on relationship formation in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of change or cultural influences on relationships.
Mentions collectivism/individualism vaguely.
Little or no relevant research used.
Response is descriptive, lacks evaluation.
Psychological terminology is inaccurate or inconsistently applied.
**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of cultural influences on relationships.
Refers to at least one relevant study (e.g., Gupta & Singh on arranged vs love marriages, Dion & Dion on cultural norms in partner choice).
Explains change in cultural practices or expectations in relationship formation.
Some evaluation of cultural bias or methodological limitations.
Conclusion present but not fully developed.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of change in cultural influences on relationships.
Accurate use of multiple studies (Gupta & Singh, Dion & Dion, Buss on mate preferences across cultures).
Explains how globalization, acculturation, or modernization create cultural change in partner selection and relationship norms.
Well-developed evaluation of strengths and limitations, with cross-cultural comparisons.
Balanced and reasoned conclusion linking change, cultural influences, and relationship formation.
Accurate and precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

questions.specification: A social psychologist interviews young adults about how they form friendships. The researcher finds that trust develops differently in collectivist and individualist cultures.

parts.content: Explain the role of cultural influence on relationships.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a relevant study and its application.

1–2 marks: Limited description of cultural influence on relationships. Minimal application to scenario.

3–4 marks: Further description of cultural influence (individualism vs. collectivism, cultural dimensions). Some accurate description of a study (e.g., aim/method). Partial application to collectivist vs. individualist trust differences.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanantion of cultural influence on relationships (purpose of marriage, cultural norms and conventions). Accurate and detailed description of a study with clear findings. Strong application to cultural influence on relationship formation.

**Acceptable responses:**
Dion & Dion (1993) — cultural values in love/marriage.
Levine et al. (1995) — cross-cultural differences in willingness to marry without love.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of causality explain the role of hormones in human social behaviour in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality or hormones (e.g., “hormones make people act”).
Mentions social behaviour vaguely (e.g., “people are aggressive or friendly”) without connection to research.
Minimal or no reference to relevant studies (e.g., Kosfeld et al., Baumgartner et al., Dabbs et al.).
Weak or no connection to human relationships.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of causality in hormonal research.
At least one relevant study explained (e.g., Kosfeld oxytocin trust games, Dabbs testosterone and aggression, Baumgartner et al. in trust vs betrayal).
Explains limitations of causality: correlation vs experimental evidence, ethical constraints on hormone manipulation.
Links to human relationships: trust, cooperation, aggression, social bonding.
Some evaluation: small sample sizes, artificial settings, confounding variables.
Conclusion present but limited.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of causality in hormonal influence on social behaviour.
Multiple studies integrated accurately (Kosfeld, Baumgartner, Dabbs, Zak).
Clear analysis: hormones can causally influence behaviour (oxytocin increasing trust), but social/environmental context interacts.
Well-developed evaluation: strengths (experimental manipulations, double-blind designs), limitations (generalizability, ethical constraints, gender/cultural differences).
Explicit link to human relationships: hormonal effects help explain trust, cooperation, and aggression patterns, but causality is complex and multi-factorial.
Balanced conclusion: biological causality is influential but not deterministic in human relationships.

questions.specification:
Andersson and Patel (2014) conducted a longitudinal study to investigate how attachment in infancy relates to later romantic relationships. The researchers were particularly interested in whether early secure attachment predicts higher relationship satisfaction in adulthood.

The original sample consisted of 200 infants assessed for attachment style at 12 months using the “Strange Situation” procedure. At age 25, participants who remained in the study (N = 92) were interviewed about their romantic relationships. Semi-structured interviews asked participants about satisfaction, trust, and conflict management.

Results showed that individuals classified as securely attached in infancy were significantly more likely to report positive and stable relationships at age 25 compared to those classified as insecurely attached. However, the researchers noted a large proportion of participants dropped out.

[Note: Fictionalised but strongly based on Hazan & Shaver’s research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, change, measurement, perspective. (Context: Human Development)
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Dropouts = attrition bias. Semi-structured interviews risk interviewer effects.
Change: Later experiences can alter attachment; not deterministic.
Measurement: Strange Situation artificial; adult interviews subjective.
Perspective: Western emphasis on romance—may not apply across cultures.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of change explain cultural influences on personal relationships in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of change or cultural influences on personal relationships.
Mentions “relationships change over time” vaguely, without clear cultural reference.
Minimal or no use of relevant research.
Weak or no link to human relationships.
Psychological terminology inaccurate or inconsistently applied.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of change in cultural influences on relationships.
At least one relevant study explained (e.g., Gupta & Singh on arranged vs. love marriages; Dion & Dion on cultural norms; Levine et al. on cultural attitudes to love).
Explains change in terms of modernization, acculturation, or globalization influencing formation/maintenance.
Some evaluation of cultural research (e.g., cultural bias, ecological validity).
Conclusion present but underdeveloped.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of change in cultural influences on personal relationships.
Multiple studies integrated (Gupta & Singh, Dion & Dion, Buss on mate preferences, Levine et al.).
Clear explanation of change:
Globalization alters cultural norms about partner choice and relationship expectations.
Acculturation shapes how immigrant populations adapt relationship practices.
Gender role changes across cultures influence formation and maintenance.
Well-developed evaluation: strengths (cross-cultural comparisons show cultural diversity and universals), limitations (risk of ethnocentrism, self-report bias, overgeneralisation).
Explicit link to human relationships: cultural change provides insight into why relationships are formed and maintained differently across societies.
Balanced conclusion: change is key, but structural/economic factors also play roles.

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of perspective apply to studies of decision-making in the context of human relationships?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of perspective or decision-making.
Mentions heuristics vaguely.
Minimal or irrelevant studies.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of perspective in decision-making.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Kahneman & Tversky on heuristics, Bechara on decision-making and the brain).
Explains perspective as cognitive vs. other viewpoints (e.g., biological).
Some evaluation but underdeveloped.
**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of perspective in decision-making.
Multiple studies (Kahneman & Tversky, Bechara et al., Loftus on biases).
Well-developed evaluation: perspectives compared (cognitive, biological, sociocultural).
Clear implications for human relationships (trust, judgments, biases in attraction/partnerships).
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of measurement applies to research on neurotransmitters in the context of human relationships.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of measurement or neurotransmitter research.
Mentions neurotransmitters (e.g., serotonin, dopamine) but with little accuracy or relevance.
Response is descriptive, lacks connection to human relationships.
Minimal or no evaluation. Psychological terminology used inaccurately or inconsistently.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of measurement and neurotransmitters.
Refers to at least one relevant study (e.g., Fisher et al. on dopamine and attraction, Crockett on serotonin and prosocial behavior).
Explains how neurotransmitters are measured indirectly (e.g., fMRI, drug administration) but lacks depth.
Some evaluation of methods (e.g., ethics of drug studies, issues of ecological validity).
Conclusion present but not fully developed.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of measurement and neurotransmitter research in human relationships.
Accurate use of multiple studies (e.g., Fisher et al. 2005, Crockett 2010, Marazziti et al.).
Clear explanation of indirect measurement (cannot measure neurotransmitters directly; relies on brain imaging, pharmacological manipulation).
Well-developed evaluation (strengths of objective techniques, limitations of ecological validity, ethics).
Accurate psychological terminology, balanced conclusion.

questions.specification:
Almeida and Cho (2017) conducted a field study to explore bystander behaviour in public spaces. Their aim was to examine whether people were more likely to help when alone or in groups, and whether cultural background influenced this tendency.

Confederates staged minor emergencies—such as dropping a stack of books or stumbling on a curb—in busy pedestrian areas. Researchers observed 500 incidents across two cities: one in a collectivist culture and one in an individualist culture. They recorded whether passersby offered help and how quickly they responded.

Findings showed that individuals who were alone were quicker to help than those in groups, supporting the “diffusion of responsibility” explanation. Cultural differences were also evident: people in the collectivist city helped more frequently overall, even in group contexts. 

[Note: Fictionalised but reflects Darley & Latané’s bystander research]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, causality, change, perspective.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Observational study lacks control; cultural stereotypes possible.
Causality: Field setting weakens cause-effect certainty.
Change: Group vs. alone conditions illustrate situational change in behaviour.
Perspective: Diffusion of responsibility interpreted differently across collectivist/individualist contexts.
